entries.summary: CERN published three variants of OHL v2 in March 2020 as the hardware analog of the permissive/weak-copyleft/strong-copyleft software stack. The rationale document (by Myriam Ayass, Andrew Katz, Javier Serrano) explains that earlier drafts attempted a single licence with options for weak copyleft, GPLv3 compatibility, and TAPR compatibility, but the text was becoming 'complex and cumbersome'. The drafters split the text into CERN-OHL-S (strongly reciprocal, successor to original CERN-OHL), CERN-OHL-W (weakly reciprocal), and CERN-OHL-P (permissive).

sources.source: CERN OHL v2 - An Introduction and Explanation (rationale document)
sources.excerpt: at one point during the drafting process for the next version we considered (and drafted) a set of exceptions designed to allow for weak copyleft, the use of proprietary library files for HDL design, and compatibility with GPLv3 and TAPR. From a legal perspective, these seemed to work, but we are conscious that the licence was becoming complex and cumbersome ... Finally, we've adopted the following names for the licences: the CERN Open Hardware Licence - Stronger Reciprocal ... Weakly Reciprocal ... and a simpler CERN Open Hardware Licence - Permissive.

entries.summary: CERN-OHL-P defines 'Source' as 'information such as design materials or digital code which can be applied to Make or test a Product' and 'Product' as 'any device, component, work or physical object ... arising from the use, application or processing of Covered Source' (SS1.2-1.4). The rationale document explains these terms were invented to avoid the horizontal and vertical boundary problems that arise when reciprocal hardware licences meet larger assemblies, since hardware has no clean software-style notion of 'file' or 'linking'.

sources.source: CERN OHL v2 - An Introduction and Explanation (rationale document)
sources.excerpt: We were conscious of the problem of defining the boundaries where the effect of the reciprocal CERN-OHL stopped. There are two issues: vertically (if I place a reciprocal CERN-OHL licensed component in another assembly, does the whole assembly need to be licensed under the equivalent reciprocal CERN-OHL, and, if so, what if that assembly is placed within an even larger item?); and horizontally: if I interface a reciprocal-licensed CERN-OHL licensed circuit board to another circuit board and distribute the two together, do both of them have to be licensed under the reciprocal CERN-OHL?

entries.summary: OSI approved CERN-OHL-P-2.0 on January 15, 2021 (submitted June 29, 2020 by Andrew Katz), alongside the -W and -S variants. The FSF license list does not classify CERN-OHL variants: it is a catalogue of 'software licenses' and explicitly states that it lists 'the most commonly encountered free software license on this page, but cannot list them all', and does not purport to classify hardware-design licences as free or non-free software.

sources.source: Open Source Initiative - CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Permissive
sources.excerpt: CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Permissive ... Version 2 ... Submitted: June 29, 2020 ... Submitter: Andrew Katz ... Approved: January 15, 2021 ... Board minutes

We try to list the most commonly encountered free software license on this page, but cannot list them all; we'll try our best to answer questions about free software licenses whether or not they are listed here.

entries.summary: The official CERN-OHL-W v2 user guide explicitly uses the CERN White Rabbit switch (a sub-nanosecond-accurate Ethernet-based timing system) as its canonical licensing example, providing boilerplate notices that point at the White Rabbit hardware sources hosted on ohwr.org. The CERN OHL home page on cern-ohl.web.cern.ch is the canonical publication point for all three variants; CERN positions itself as 'custodian of the CERN OHL' and releases new versions and variants from time to time.

sources.excerpt:
Source location: https://www.ohwr.org/project/wr-switch-hw
As per CERN-OHL-W v2 section 4.1, should You produce hardware based on these sources, You must maintain the Source Location visible on the external case of the White Rabbit switch or other product you make using this documentation.

CERN has developed the CERN Open Hardware Licence (CERN OHL) as a legal tool to promote collaboration among hardware designers and support the freedom to use, study, modify, share and distribute hardware designs, and products based on those designs. As the custodian of the CERN OHL, CERN releases new versions and variants from time to time to address new problems or concerns ... The current version is version 2 ...

sources.source: CERN OHL v2 - An Introduction and Explanation (rationale document)
Open Source Initiative - CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Permissive
